questions.question_text: In Alaska, who is ultimately responsible for the activities of all licensees operating under a brokerage?
distractors: Each individual salesperson
The Alaska Real Estate Commission
The associate broker in charge
questions.explanation: The broker is responsible for the activities of all licensees under their supervision. This includes ensuring compliance with statutes and regulations. While individual licensees have personal obligations, the broker bears the supervisory responsibility.

questions.question_text: A brokerage opens a branch office in Fairbanks. Who must be assigned to supervise that office?
questions.correct_answer: An associate broker whose principal place is that office
distractors: Any licensed salesperson with 5+ years experience
The principal broker from the main office
A licensed office manager without a broker license
questions.explanation: Alaska requires that each branch office be under the direct supervision of an associate broker whose principal place of business is that office. This ensures on-site supervision and compliance at each location.

questions.question_text: An associate broker is asked to supervise two branch offices simultaneously. Is this permitted under Alaska law?
questions.correct_answer: No, an associate broker may supervise only one office
distractors: Yes, if both offices are within 50 miles
Yes, if the broker approves in writing
No, only the principal broker can supervise branch offices
questions.explanation: An associate broker may serve as direct supervisor at only one office. This limitation ensures that each branch office receives adequate supervision. The associate broker's principal place of business must be the office they supervise.

questions.question_text: A salesperson changes brokerages. What must happen regarding supervision?
questions.correct_answer: The new broker assumes supervisory responsibility
distractors: AREC must approve the transfer within 30 days
The old broker remains liable for 6 months
The salesperson operates independently during transition
questions.explanation: When a salesperson changes brokers, the new employing broker assumes supervisory responsibility. The salesperson cannot practice without broker supervision — there is no independent practice period during the transition.

questions.question_text: A broker discovers that a salesperson under their supervision has been commingling trust funds. What is the broker's obligation?
questions.correct_answer: Take immediate corrective action and ensure compliance
distractors: Simply report the issue to AREC and wait
Terminate the salesperson but take no other action
Allow the salesperson 30 days to correct the issue
questions.explanation: The broker has a duty to ensure compliance with statutes and regulations by all supervised licensees. Upon discovering a violation like commingling, the broker must take immediate corrective action. Failure to act could result in disciplinary action against the broker.

questions.question_text: A salesperson receives a written offer from a buyer. What must the salesperson do with the offer?
questions.correct_answer: Promptly submit it to the seller
distractors: Hold it for 24 hours to gather competing offers
Submit it only if the offer is at or above list price
Forward it to the broker for review before the seller
questions.explanation: Timely presentation of all written offers is a duty owed in every licensee relationship under AS 08.88.615(a)(3), and AS 08.88.625 does not allow that duty to be waived. Failing to submit written bona fide offers is grounds for discipline under 12 AAC 64.130. A salesperson cannot hold, filter, or delay offers.

questions.question_text: After a purchase agreement is signed by both parties, what must the licensee do with copies?
questions.correct_answer: Provide copies of all signed documents to all parties
distractors: Keep copies only in the brokerage file
Provide copies only to the party they represent
File copies with AREC within 10 business days
questions.explanation: The licensee must provide copies of all signed documents to all parties to the transaction. This ensures everyone has complete records and can refer to the terms of the agreement. There is no requirement to file copies with AREC.

questions.question_text: How long must an Alaska broker maintain transaction records?
distractors: A minimum of 5 years
A minimum of 7 years
A minimum of 1 year
questions.explanation: All transaction records — including listing agreements, purchase contracts, trust account records, correspondence, and disclosures — must be maintained for a minimum of 3 years. Records must be available for Commission examination at any time.

questions.question_text: A seller instructs the listing agent in writing not to present any offers below $400,000. A buyer submits an offer for $380,000. What should the agent do?
questions.correct_answer: Present the offer to the seller despite the instruction
distractors: Honor the written instruction and withhold the offer
Reject the offer on behalf of the seller
Hold the offer until a higher offer arrives
questions.explanation: Timely presentation of all written offers is a duty owed in every licensee relationship under AS 08.88.615(a)(3), and AS 08.88.625 does not allow the parties to waive it — even by written instruction. Failing to submit a written bona fide offer before the seller accepts another offer is grounds for license suspension or revocation under 12 AAC 64.130(13).

questions.question_text: AREC requests to examine a broker's transaction records from 18 months ago. Can the broker refuse?
questions.correct_answer: No, records must be available for Commission examination
distractors: Yes, AREC needs a court order for records
Yes, if no complaint has been filed
No, but only within the first 12 months
questions.explanation: The Commission may examine broker records at any time. Records must be maintained for 3 years and be available for examination. There is no requirement for a complaint or court order — the Commission has the authority to audit records proactively.

questions.question_text: A buyer gives a salesperson a $10,000 earnest money check. Where must this money be deposited?
questions.correct_answer: The broker's trust account at a federally insured bank
distractors: The salesperson's personal business checking account
Any bank account of the buyer's choosing
The listing agent's individual escrow account
questions.explanation: Trust funds must be deposited in the broker's trust account at a federally insured financial institution in Alaska. The salesperson cannot deposit funds in a personal account — all trust funds flow through the broker's designated trust account.

questions.question_text: Under what circumstances may an Alaska broker deposit trust funds later than 5 days after receiving them?
questions.correct_answer: When geography, weather, or transportation makes timely deposit impossible or unreasonable
distractors: Whenever the purchase agreement allows a longer period
Only with written permission from AREC
Never, the deadline has no exceptions
questions.explanation: Under 12 AAC 64.200, money received in trust must be deposited in or mailed to the appropriate trust account within 5 days following receipt. The regulation excuses a later deposit only when factors such as geographical location, weather conditions, or transportation facilities make depositing within that time impossible or unreasonable — a distinctly Alaskan allowance.

questions.question_text: A broker deposits a client's earnest money into the brokerage's general operating account. What violation has occurred?
questions.correct_answer: Commingling trust funds with business funds
distractors: Conversion of trust funds for personal use
Embezzlement of client property deposits
Unauthorized practice of banking activities
questions.explanation: Commingling — mixing trust funds with personal or business funds — is prohibited in Alaska. Trust funds must be kept in a designated trust account, separate from the brokerage's operating funds. This protects clients' money from the broker's business obligations.

questions.question_text: Who may disburse funds from an Alaska brokerage trust account?
questions.correct_answer: The broker or an authorized designee
distractors: Any licensed salesperson in the brokerage
The buyer or seller upon written request
Only the title company handling the closing
questions.explanation: Only the broker or a person specifically authorized by the broker may disburse trust funds. This control mechanism ensures accountability and prevents unauthorized distribution of funds held in trust for clients.

questions.question_text: A buyer's earnest money check is delivered to the listing broker. Under Alaska regulation, when must the broker deposit it into the trust account?
distractors: Within a reasonable time determined by the broker
Within 24 hours of receipt
At the time of closing only
questions.explanation: 12 AAC 64.200 requires money received in trust to be deposited in or mailed to the appropriate trust account within 5 days following receipt. The only excuse for a later deposit is when geographical location, weather conditions, or transportation facilities make meeting the deadline impossible or unreasonable.
